tolerance.notes: Coefficient ASTs are simple rational expressions in (R, dlon, dlat, cos_lat); only `cos` is transcendental and is libm-stable across Linux/macOS to well under 1e-14. The Julia evaluator is the reference per docs/GRIDS_API.md §4.3; the Python evaluator must match to this tolerance.

reference_binding_notes: ESD ships a Julia rule evaluator (src/rule_eval.jl, a thin passthrough to EarthSciSerialization.evaluate). Rust + TypeScript siblings will follow once the Python pilot lands.

coefficient_cases.name: unit_sphere_equator
coefficient_cases.notes: Unit sphere (R=1), 1° spacing, lat=0 -> cos_lat=1. Exercises the simplest baseline.

coefficient_cases.name: earth_60N
coefficient_cases.notes: Earth radius, 1° spacing, lat=60° -> cos_lat=0.5. Exercises non-unit R and the lon metric.

coefficient_cases.name: anisotropic_high_lat
coefficient_cases.notes: Anisotropic dlon != dlat, high latitude (lat=80° -> cos_lat=0.17365). Exercises the lon-axis cos_lat metric.

stencil_cases.name: small_grid_sin_lon_cos_lat
stencil_cases.notes: 8x16 (nlat x nlon) grid on the unit sphere with f(lon,lat)=sin(lon)*cos(lat). Apply the rule at each interior cell (no lat-axis wrap) and store the per-cell stencil sums for both axes' contributions. The lon stencil approximates d_lon/(R cos_lat) of f; the lat stencil approximates d_lat/R of f.
